title: The Wisdom of Crowds: Ensemble Learning
concept: Ensemble learning is a machine learning strategy that combines the predictions of multiple individual models to create a single, more accurate, and robust prediction.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is decision tree pruning?

options.text: Trimming back branches that don't add generalizable information to prevent overfitting.
Adding new training examples to the leaf nodes.
Changing categorical splits into numerical ones.

exercises.explain: Correct! Pruning trims the tree to keep it simple and robust.

cards.title: The weight of the ox
cards.body: In 1906, statistician Francis Galton watched 800 people guess the weight of an ox at a county fair. Individual guesses were all over the place. But when he averaged all 800 guesses, the crowd's average was 1,197 pounds — within 1 pound of the true weight! The crowd as a whole was smarter than almost every single expert.

cards.title: Ensemble Learning: Strength in numbers
cards.body: Ensemble learning is the strategy of training multiple models (called base learners) and combining their predictions. Instead of relying on one 'perfect' model, we let a crowd of models vote or average their answers to get a more stable prediction.

cards.title: Asking for second opinions
cards.body: If you face a major medical decision, you don't just ask one doctor. You ask a panel of five doctors. If four out of five agree on a treatment, you feel much more confident. Each doctor has their own perspective, and combining their knowledge reduces the chance of a single doctor making a mistake.

cards.title: The voting committee
cards.body: To predict if a stock will rise, we train 3 different models. Model A says Yes. Model B says Yes. Model C says No. The ensemble aggregates their votes: 2 vs 1. The final ensemble prediction is Yes. By voting, the ensemble corrected Model C's individual mistake.

cards.title: Diverse models make better crowds
cards.body: You might assume an ensemble works best if all its models are trained identically. In reality, a crowd is only smart if its members are **diverse**. If all 5 doctors studied the exact same slides at the same school, they will make the same mistakes. We need models that look at different parts of the data to build a strong ensemble.

cards.title: Single Model vs. Ensemble
cards.body: Comparing a single predictor to an ensemble:

left.text:
One decision maker.
Fast, but sensitive to errors and noise.
If it makes a mistake, the prediction fails.

right.text:
Many models voting together.
Slightly slower, but highly robust.
Individual mistakes are outvoted.

exercises.prompt: What is the core strategy of ensemble learning?

options.text: Combining the predictions of multiple models to create a single, more robust prediction.
Training a single model for a much longer period of time.
Removing all features from the dataset to simplify calculations.

exercises.explain: Correct! Ensemble learning combines multiple models to improve prediction accuracy.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An ensemble of models works best when every single model in the committee makes identical predictions on the training data.
exercises.explain: False! Ensembles need diversity — if all models are identical, they will make the same errors, providing no benefit over a single model.

exercises.sentence: Ensemble learning combines predictions from multiple individual models, which are often called base ___.
exercises.explain: The individual models that make up an ensemble are called base learners.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario represents an ensemble model?

options.text: An app routes a classification task to 10 decision trees, then outputs the category that received the most votes.
An app routes a classification task to one decision tree with 10 levels of depth.
An app uses a single linear regression equation to predict a housing price.

exercises.explain: Correct! Allowing 10 separate trees to vote on the output is ensemble learning.

exercises.prompt: You build an ensemble of 5 models to predict if a credit card transaction is fraud. Model 1, 2, and 3 predict 'Fraud'. Model 4 and 5 predict 'Safe'. How does the ensemble arrive at its final output, and what is it?

options.text: It aggregates the votes (3 vs 2), outputting 'Fraud' based on majority rule.
It averages the models' internal parameters and prints 'Safe'.
It selects the output of Model 5 because it was the last one trained.
It rejects the transaction because the models disagreed.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The ensemble aggregates the votes of its base learners. Since the majority vote (3 out of 5) was for 'Fraud', that becomes the final prediction. Next, let's learn how we combine these votes for different tasks!
